sentences.grammarNote: Je m'appelle uses the reflexive verb s'appeler — literally 'I call myself'. It is the most natural way to give your name in French, far more common than mon nom est.

sentences.base_language: I am American, but I live in Paris.
sentences.target_language: Je suis américain, mais j'habite à Paris.
sentences.grammarNote:
**américain** (lowercase) — nationalities used as adjectives are not capitalised in French: il est américain, elle est française, nous sommes japonais.
Capitalise only when the nationality is used as a noun: un Américain (an American person).
**j'habite à Paris** = I live in Paris — **habiter** + **à** for cities: j'habite à Lyon, à Tokyo. Use **en** for countries: j'habite en France.

Apprendre (to learn) is an irregular verb conjugated like prendre: j'apprends, tu apprends, il apprend, nous apprenons. Note that le français has a lowercase f — languages are not capitalised in French.

sentences.target_language: Le français est une belle langue.
sentences.grammarNote:
**belle** = beautiful (feminine form of **beau**); **beau** → **belle** before feminine nouns.
**beau** is a BANGS adjective that comes before the noun: une **belle** langue (not *une langue belle*).
**la langue** = language/tongue (feminine): une belle langue, des langues étrangères.

**c'est** = it is — used for general statements about things or situations. Compare: **il est** (used before adjective + noun: il est professeur) vs **c'est** (used before adjective + thing: c'est difficile, c'est bon).
**aussi** = also/too — placed after the verb in French (not at the beginning like in English): c'est aussi vrai.

sentences.base_language: I practice a little every day.
sentences.target_language: Je pratique un peu tous les jours.
sentences.grammarNote: Tous les jours (every day) uses tous with the masculine plural les jours. Compare: toutes les semaines (every week, feminine). The s in tous is pronounced here because it modifies a noun.

sentences.base_language: I really want to visit France.
sentences.target_language: J'ai vraiment envie de visiter la France.
sentences.grammarNote: Avoir envie de + infinitive means 'to want to / feel like doing' something. It is often more natural than vouloir in everyday speech: j'ai envie de dormir, tu as envie de sortir ?

sentences.base_language: I would love to see the Eiffel Tower.
sentences.grammarNote:
**j'adorerais** = I would love to — conditional tense of **adorer**. The conditional is formed by adding imperfect endings to the infinitive: -ais, -ais, -ait, -ions, -iez, -aient.
**la tour Eiffel** — note lowercase **tour**: in French, landmarks use lowercase for the common noun (la tour, le pont, le château) and uppercase for the proper name.

sentences.base_language: And I want to eat croissants every morning.
sentences.target_language: Et je veux manger des croissants tous les matins.
sentences.grammarNote:
**des croissants** = croissants (indefinite plural) — **des** is the plural of **un/une**.
After a negative, **des** becomes **de**: je ne veux pas manger **de** croissants.
**tous les matins** = every morning — **tous les** + masculine plural noun: tous les jours, tous les soirs, tous les matins.

**Vous parlez français ?** — question by intonation, no inversion needed in spoken French.
Note: **parler français** (no article) — languages after **parler** take no article: parler français, parler anglais, parler espagnol.
But with other verbs, use the article: j'apprends **le** français, j'aime **l'**anglais.

sentences.base_language: A little, but I am still learning!
sentences.grammarNote:
**encore** = still/yet (in this positive context, 'still learning') or again/more.
In a negative sentence, **encore** means 'yet': je n'ai pas encore fini (I haven't finished yet).
In a positive sentence: j'apprends encore (I'm still learning) · **encore une fois** (once more/again).
